quotes.text: Aaron and Miriam in their declining years seem to have objected to the idea that Moses was the sole spokesman for the Lord, and they justified their fault-finding by berating him for his marriage in Egypt to an Ethiopian. Josephus (Antiquities of the Jews 2:10:2) indeed recorded an account of such a marriage, proposed by the princess and the king of Ethiopia for military and political reasons, but the Bible mentions it nowhere else. That marriage is not to be confused with his marriage in Midian to Zipporah, daughter of the high priest Jethro (Ex. 2:15-22). Note Moses' humility under criticism (Num. 12:3; cf. Matt. 11:29). The fault-finding by Miriam and Aaron against the Lord's anointed was wrong, and He chastised them. Moses pleaded that Miriam be healed, and she was.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 153

quotes.text: Miriam and Aaron spoke against The verb va-tedabber is in the feminine singular, indicating that Miriam was the principal instigator of the gossip (also note that Miriam is mentioned ahead of Aaron, the reverse of the normal order; cf. vv. 4,5). This would therefore account for the fact that she and not Aaron was punished.' Singular verbs are also used with Moses and Deborah, the authors of their songs, even though their names are followed by "the Israelites" and "Barak," respectively (Exod. 15:1; Judg. 5:1). And similarly with Esther: Although she is coupled with Mordecai, it is she who is the principal author of the decree to observe Purim (Esther 9:29; cf. v. 32).
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 94

quotes.text: Joseph Smith "spoke of the disposition of many men to consider the lower offices in the Church dishonorable, and to look with jealous eyes upon the standing of others who are called to preside over them; that it was the folly and nonsense of the human heart for a person to be aspiring to other stations than those to which they are appointed of God for them to occupy; that it was better for individuals to magnify their respective calling, and wait patiently till God shall say to them, 'Come up higher."'
quotes.attr: Teachings, 223-224

quotes.text: The prophets, seers, and revelators have had and still have the responsibility and privilege of receiving and declaring the word of God for the world. Individual members, parents, and leaders have the right to receive revelation for their own responsibility but have no duty nor right to declare the word of God beyond the limits of their own responsibility.
quotes.speaker: James E. Faust
quotes.attr: James E. Faust, Ensign, Nov 1989, p8

quotes.text: There is the man who, to satisfy his own egotism, took a stand against the authorities of the Church. He followed the usual pattern, not apostasy at first, only superiority of knowledge and mild criticism. He loved the brethren, he said, but they failed to see and interpret as he would like. He would still love the Church, he maintained, but his criticism grew and developed into ever-widening circles. He was right, he assured himself; he could not yield in good conscience; he had his pride. His children did not accept his philosophy wholly, but their confidence was shaken. In their frustration, they married out of the Church, and he lost them. He later realized his folly and returned to humbleness, but so very late. He had lost his children…. 'The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge.' (Ezekiel 18:2.)
quotes.speaker: Teachings of Spencer W. Kimball
quotes.attr: Teachings of Spencer W. Kimball, p184

quotes.text: The LORD heard it But not Moses, according to Shadal. The consequence of the Lord's hearing is self-understood and is more powerful by the text's deliberate silence on the matter than if it had put it in words (similarly, cf. Gen. 35:22). The expected result, God's anger, is withheld until verse 9 (cf. 11:1); the punishment then follows.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 94

quotes.text: Herein lies the true reason for Miriam and Aaron's complaint; the previous one was only a pretext. What they were really after was a share in Moses' leadership.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 94

quotes.text: One is not truly converted until he sees the power of God resting upon the leaders of this Church, and until it goes down into his heart like fire.
quotes.speaker: Harold B. Lee
quotes.attr: Harold B. Lee, Stand Ye In Holy Places, p63

quotes.text: Reflective of our total progression, repentance is not solely for renouncing transgression. For instance, Moses was a righteous and remarkable man. Nevertheless, he needed to change his leadership style for his welfare as well as the people’s (see Exodus 18:17–19). Moses succeeded because he was the most meek man upon the face of the earth (see Numbers 12:3). Blessed are the meek, for they are neither easily offended by counsel nor aggravated by admonition. If we were more meek, brothers and sisters, repentance would be much more regular and less stared at.
quotes.speaker: Neal A. Maxwell
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Oct. 1991, 42; or Ensign, Nov. 1991, 32

quotes.text: Moses was described as the most meek man upon the face of the earth. (See Numbers 12:3.) Yet Moses had a brief moment when he rashly declared, “Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?” [CR, 10] (Numbers 20:10; italics added). Even so, the Lord readied remarkable Moses [Ensign, 10] for later service, including that atop the Mount of Transfiguration. (See Matthew 17:1–4.)
quotes.speaker: Neal A. Maxwell
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Oct. 1984, 9–10; or Ensign, Nov. 1984, 9–10

quotes.text: At a place called Meribah, one of the greatest ever, Moses, was fatigued by people clamoring for water. Momentarily, Moses “spake unadvisedly,” saying, “Must we fetch you water?” (Psalm 106:33; Numbers 20:10; see also Deuteronomy 4:21). The Lord mentored remarkable Moses through the pronoun problem and further magnified him. We would do well to be as meek as Moses (see Numbers 12:3).
quotes.speaker: Neal A. Maxwell
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Apr. 2002, 43; or Ensign, May 2002, 37

quotes.text: Most of us who are called to leadership in the Church feel that we are inadequate because of inexperience, lack of ability, or meager learning and education. Of the many descriptions of Moses is the following: “Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth” (Num. 12:3 [Numbers 12:3]).
quotes.speaker: James E. Faust
quotes.attr: James E. Faust, in Conference Report, Oct. 1980, 52; or Ensign, Nov. 1980, 36

quotes.text: When the Lord returns it will be in light and glory, here described as a cloud. His coming is also described as "a pillar of fire" (D&C 29:12). Of his appearances anciently, we read that he "came down in a cloud" (Numbers 11:25) or "in the pillar of the cloud" (Numbers 12:5). Likewise, when he spoke with the brother of Jared, he "stood in a cloud" (Ether 2:14). The experiences of Peter, James, and John on the Mount of Transfiguration were described by Matthew as a "bright cloud" (Matthew 17:5). Hebrew sources refer to the glory that attends the Lord as "The Presence" or the Shechinah.
quotes.attr: Craig J. Ostler and Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie, Revelations of the Restoration

notes: 6-8. These verses are poetry (and probably originate in an ancient epic concerning Moses). This is apparent when they are typographically reset" They reveal an introverted structure.22 The number of syllables per line is indicated in parentheses. Alterations of the translation are explained in the Commentary and notes. Introduction: Hear these My words (7)

quotes.text: It is in chapter 12 that we gain a keener insight into Moses' character, an insight obtained first from a verse presumably written by Moses himself: "Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth" (Num. 12:3). The Lord descended in a cloud, summoned Miriam and Aaron before him, and continued to expand our understanding of Moses' greatness and unusual prophetic stature: "If there be a prophet among you, I the Lord will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream" (Num. 12:6). One would suppose that such divine manifestations (visions or dreams) on a regular basis would be commendable and laudatory, even for prophets. But Moses was unusual: "With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold" (Num. 12:8). All revelation is marvelous, but some is more direct than others. In some cases "the Spirit speaketh expressly" (1 Tim. 4:1), implying that sometimes it does not speak quite so clearly. Joseph Smith spoke of the "still small voice, which whispereth through and pierceth all things, and often times it maketh my bones to quake while it maketh manifest" (D&C 85:6). As it is with revelation, so it is with prophets: some prophets enjoy a greater intimacy with the Lord than others. Moses spoke with the Lord face to face (cf. Moses 1:2; Ex. 33:11).
quotes.attr: Robert L. Millet in Kent P. Jackson and Robert L. Millet, eds., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 3: Genesis to 2 Samuel, p.189-190

quotes.text: A. If either of you is YHVH's prophet (9) B. I make Myself known to him in a vision (8) C. I speak with him in a dream (7) D. Not so with My servant Moses (6) D'. He (alone) is trusted in all My household (7) C. With him I speak mouth to mouth (7) B'. Plainly and not in riddles (8) A'. And he beholds the likeness of the Lord (7) Conclusion: How then did you not shrink (7) from speaking against My servant Moses. (9) This introverted structure clarifies the point of the poem. The first half describes God's communication with other prophets (ABC), the second half, His unique transmission to Moses (C'B'A'). The pivot sets Moses apart from his prophetic counterparts (D) by declaring that God confides in him alone (D').
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 95

quotes.text: The doctrine that man is created in the image of God was also lost in the apostasy. The vision given to Joseph Smith restored the true doctrine in relation to this question. It is just as strange that man, in his spiritual darkness, would change this glorious doctrine and in these later times substitute for it the abominable doctrine that man has ascended through countless ages from lower forms of life, as it is that they could make of the Father and the Son and the Holy Ghost a God to be worshipped that is without substance, immaterial and therefore non-existent. Here are a few references showing the error of the teaching of the so-called Christian world, all creeds and denominations, on this point, and proving that God has both body, parts and passions: Gen. 1:26. 5:1 and 9:6. James 3:8-9. Exodus 33:9-23. Numbers 12:7-8. Ex. 20:5. Deut. 4:24.) The Savior being God-the Son, and a member of the Godhead-as believed by most denominations, and since he had a body, and that body was tangible after his resurrection; then we have definite proof that God has a body of flesh and bones as tangible as man's. His disciples handled him after his resurrection; he ate in their presence (Luke 24:38-44). He has promised to return with that same body and reveal it, even showing the prints of nails in his hands to the unbelieving Jews (Zech. 12:10. 13:6. 14:4. Compare D. and C. 45:49-53). Since there are innumerable passages proving this doctrine, have members of the class present as many as they can.
quotes.speaker: Joseph Fielding Smith
quotes.attr: Joseph Fielding Smith, Church History and Modern Revelation, 4 vols., 1:, p.11

quotes.text: He made himself known by various means of communication to Moses, who even beheld the “similitude” of God (see Numbers 12:8); and this account of direct communication has been preserved by Israel through all generations.
quotes.speaker: N. Eldon Tanner
quotes.attr: N. Eldon Tanner, in Conference Report, Oct. 1978, 69; or Ensign, Nov. 1978, 47

quotes.text: Moses stands preeminent above all Israel's prophets. "With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches," the Lord said, "and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold." (Num. 12:8.) Hence we read: "And the Lord spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend." (Ex. 33:11.) And: "Moses stood in the presence of God, and talked with him face to face." (Moses 1:31.) After he was taken from the midst of Israel, the account attests: "And there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face." (Deut. 34:10.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.source: The Promised Messiah: The First Coming of Christ
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Promised Messiah: The First Coming of Christ, p.601

quotes.text: Still incensed with them Literally, "The Lord was incensed with them," referring back to verse 2, when God heard Miriam and Aaron murmuring against Moses (cf. 11:1 and Excursus 24). The displacement of the anger to verse 9 means that God's anger did not abate but only mounted during the confrontation. The literal rendering also makes sense if it is understood as an expression of divine punishment. Thus, "The Lord heard it" (v. 2) implies His anger, and "The Lord was incensed with them" (v. 9) implies His punishment.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 96

quotes.text: As the cloud withdrew The use of the perfect preceded by the subject breaks the narrative sequence, thus equating the Lord (v. 9) with the cloud. A midrash informs us that God's withdrawal was a sign of his love and compassion. It is like a king who tells the teacher: Discipline my son, but only after I leave.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 96

quotes.text: We lament that leaders of both groups had to endure dissention from their close associates. On occasion, Moses encountered opposition from his beloved Aaron and Miriam (see Numbers 12:2-11). Latter-day leaders also suffered contention among their trusted associates (see History of the Church, 1:104-5, 226).
quotes.speaker: Russell M. Nelson
quotes.attr: Russell M. Nelson, "The Exodus Repeated", CES Fireside for Young Adults, 7 September 1997

notes: Let her not be as one dead In antiquity, the leper was regarded as a dead person. The Septuagint adds

quotes.text: Moses is caught in a dilemma. On the one hand, Miriam had openly spoken against him and deserved to be punished. On the other hand, as his sister, she evoked his compassion. Moses' terse prayer-bereft of emotion and substantiation-is the result. It is a model of brevity, perhaps lest he be accused of showing favoritism to his sister. It is more likely, however, that the prayer's brevity indicates Moses' lack of enthusiasm and minimal compliance with Aaron's plea, an attitude supported by the fact that Miriam is not referred to by name but by the impersonal third person. The prayer's structure is a near perfect introversion: 'el na' refa' na' lab, ABXB'A'. The key word refa', "heal," is the pivotal center X, BB' contain the identical word, and AA' are monosyllables consisting of the same voiced consonant, lamed.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 98

notes: The fact that leprosy is not ordinarily communicable by mere outward contact is accentuated by Trench, Notes on the Miracles, pp. 165-168, and the isolation of lepers required by the Mosaic law is regarded by him as an intended object lesson and figure to illustrate spiritual uncleanness. He says: "I refer to the mistaken assumption that leprosy was catching from one person to another; and that the lepers were so carefully secluded from their fellowmen lest they might communicate the disease to others, as in like manner that the torn garment, the covered lip, the cry, 'Unclean, unclean' (Lev. 13:45) were warnings to all that they should keep aloof, lest unawares touching a leper, or drawing unto too great a nearness, they should become partakers of this disease. So far from any danger of the kind existing, nearly all who have looked closest into the matter agree that the sickness was incommunicable by ordinary contact from one person to another. A leper might transmit it to his children, or the mother of a leper's children might take it from him; but it was by no ordinary contact communicable from one person to another. All the notices in the Old Testament, as well as in other Jewish books, confirm the statement that we have here something very much higher than a mere sanitary regulation. Thus, when the law of Moses was not observed, no such exclusion necessarily found place; Naaman the leper commanded the armies of Syria (2 Kgs. 5:1) Gehazi, with his leprosy that never should be cleansed (2 Kgs. 5:27); talked familiarly with the king of apostate Israel (2 Kgs. 8:5). . . . How, moreover, should the Levitical priests, had the disease been this creeping infection, have ever themselves escaped it, obliged as they were by their very office to submit the leper to actual handling and closest examination? . . . Leprosy was nothing short of a living death, a corrupting of all the humors, a poisoning of the very springs, of life; a dissolution, little by little, of the whole body, so that one limb after another actually decayed and fell away. Aaron exactly describes the appearance which the leper presented to the eyes of the beholders, when, pleading for Miriam, he says, 'Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb.' (Num. 12:12.) The disease, moreover, was incurable by the art and skill of man; not that the leper might not return to health; for, however rare, such cases are contemplated in the Levitical law. . . . The lepor, thus fearfully bearing about the body the outward and visible tokens of sin in the

people did not march Out of respect for Miriam, because Miriam had watched over Moses at the river bank for a short while.57 A more cogent reason, however, is that all of Israel pays a penalty for Miriam's sin: Their march to the promised land must be delayed a full week. (Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah

NUMBERS 13 & 14 - They sent 12 spies into the land of Canaan from the wilderness of Paran. Two became important; Caleb and Oshea, of Judah and Ephriam. Before Moses sent Oshea into the promised land, he renamed him JOSHUA. He put the "yod" in front of his name, making is Jah. He had seen that Joshua was extra ordinary. He saw him as a type of Christ. In Greek, Joshua is JESUS. He ends up taking the Children of Israel into the Promised land. As a type, he goes to survey the land. Not for 39 years does he get to take them into that land. They were gone 40 days, of course. Upon their return, ten said how great it was, but complained that they could never take it. This became known as the "evil report." They focused on the people in the land, and their strong forts and cities.

quotes.text: Joshua and Caleb said that they should go up at once and possess it. He knew they could overcome it, with God's help. But the 10 spies said no, it couldn't be done. There were giants, and we were as grasshoppers. This was tough on Israel. They had come a long way, tough time, the majority of their spies, personally selected by Moses, said we can't make it. They felt they were stuck in the desert, between Egypt and Canaan, with no place to go. They wept all night. They murmured against Moses the next day. They consider returning to Egypt. Moses and Aaron fall on their faces, Caleb and Joshua rend their clothes. They asked Israel not to rebel against the Lord, but trust in him. Don't fear the people of the land, "for they are bread for us;" Keep the covenant. God will fight for us. But reality got in the way. The congregation wanted to stone them. God appears to Moses and says he will smite them all, and make a greater nation of Moses alone. Moses then turns mediator. He says that if God kills them all, that the Egyptians will say, what kind of God is this, to take out his people, and then kill them all in the desert? It will look like you can't do what you set out to do. (see 14:13-24) What is happening here? Really, Moses is allowed to fulfill the similitude of Christ, as a mediator and advocate for Israel. God wasn't about the wipe them all out, but he allowed Moses to feel deeply about the people, and mediate for them. Then God promised that his glory would fill the earth, but those who wouldn't hearken to his voice, wouldn't go into the land. Caleb and Joshua would. The others broke the covenant, and so their CARCASSES would cover the wilderness, and you will not enter the LAND. Everyone who rejected the covenant would die. The 10 spies died by the plague. 14:37. Notice the reaction in Israel. Like a bunch of little kids. They mourned greatly, and the next day, lined up on the top of the mountain to go into the Promised Land. Moses said, no you are not. Now if you go you cannot prosper, for you broke the covenant. Before, you would have prospered in the land. Now you can't. God won't go with you.
quotes.speaker: Robert Norman
quotes.attr: Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes

quotes.text: According to the Torah, the wilderness period was marked by two egregious sins: the apostasy of the golden calf (Exod. 32-34) and the faithlessness of the scouts (Num. 13-14). Only these two sins are singled out for special mention in the survey of the wilderness trek given by Deuteronomy (1:22-45; 9:12-25), and only in connection with these two sins does God threaten the annihilation of Israel and the fulfillment of the patriarchal promise through Moses (Exod. 32:10; Num. 14:12). Echoes of the scout episode resonate throughout Scripture. The story of Israel's rebellion is artfully constructed. The scouts' factual but negative report (13:28-29) sets off a wave of murmuring (13:3oa): The exaggerated description of the dangers (13:32-33) leads to organized vocal opposition (14:1-3) and a threat to return to Egypt under new leadership (14:4-10). The juxtaposition of chapters 13-14 with chapter 12 derives from the tradition that Israel's rebellion following the reconnaissance of Canaan took place in the wilderness of Paran (12:16; 13:26). There also is a psychological connection: Miriam and Aaron's public outburst against Moses (I2:I-2,8b) may have encouraged the malcontents among the Israelites to do the same. Shelah-lekha Moses, bidden by God to send scouts to reconnoiter the land, chooses twelve chieftains, one from each tribe, and gives them specific questions to investigate concerning the land and its inhabitants. Their names differ from the tribal chieftains previously listed in chapters I-2:7; whereas the census and altar initiation had called for the most senior leaders, a mission of arduous and dangerous reconnaissance requires younger leaders.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 99

quotes.text: Because of the faithlessness of most members of the reconnaissance expedition to Canaan, Israel is condemned to spend forty years in the wilderness, mostly in the area of Kadesh-barnea (see Map I), where they receive new laws (chaps. 15, 18-19) and where the authority of Aaron and Moses (chaps. 16-17) and even God (20:1-13) is flouted.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 99

quotes.text: Paran That is, at Kadesh (cf. Num. 13:26; Deut. 1:19), according to Ramban.
quotes.speaker: Jacob Milgrom
quotes.attr: Jacob Milgrom, The JPS Torah Commentary: Numbers, Philadelphia, 1989, 99
